content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5652          322-5560               322-5901               322-6441          322-6444


December 27, 1979


Edmund A. Duggan
City Attorney
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA  94902

Dear Mr. Duggan:

     Thank you for your letter of December 7, 1979, requesting
advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act.  This advice letter, provided pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114(b), will confirm the results
of our telephone conversation.

     The facts, as stated in your letter and in our conversa-
tions, are as follows:  Cecilia Bridges is a Planning Commis-
sioner for San Rafael.  By training, Mrs. Bridges is a marine
biologist; by profession, she is an environmental consultant.
Among her clients is Madrone Associates from whom she received
more than $250 income within the last 12 months.  Madrone
Associates performs biological investigations for clients,
including government agencies, and is presently performing
consulting services for the City of San Rafael, the results
of which will comprise part of an application to be acted
upon by the Planning Commission.  Mrs. Bridges has not pro-
vided any services to Madrone in connection with this project
and has never undertaken any project for Madrone which con-
cerned the City.  Madrone Associates was chosen by the City
as a consultant because it possesses the kind of biological
expertise required for this project which involves study of
wetlands.  Madrone's contract is with the City itself, not
with the developer, although, as is common practice with
preparation of environmental impact reports, the developer
must pay the costs of the study.  In general, consultants
are chosen by the City on the basis of objective criteria
such as their expertise and their capacity for a particular
job.

Edmund A. Duggan
December 27, 1979
Page Two


In light of these facts, your question is: When a
matter for which Madrone Associates has provided services
comes before the Planning Commission, is Mrs. Bridges dis-
qualified even though she has not herself provided any ser-
vices to Madrone in connection with that matter?

The disqualification provision of Government Code Section
87100 provides:

     No public official at any level of state or local
     government shall make, participate in making or in
     any way attempt to use his official position to
     influence a governmental decision in which he
     knows or has reason to know he has a financial
     interest.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within
the meaning of the preceding section, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect upon the public
generally, on

     any source of income ... aggregating two hundred
     fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by
     or promised to the public official within twelve
     months prior to the time when the decision is
     made...

                         Government Code Section 87103(c).

Because Mrs. Bridges derived more than $250 income from
Madrone Associates within the preceding 12 months, the ques-
tion of her disqualification turns upon whether a decision
made by her in her capacity as a Planning Commissioner will
have a material financial effect on her or upon Madrone
Associates.

By regulation, the Commission has specified certain
factors to be taken into consideration in determining whether
an official decision will have a material financial effect.
These factors are contained in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702. Pertinent to the situation under consideration are:
whether the effect of the decision will be to directly in-
crease or decrease Mrs. Bridges' income by $100 or more (2 Cal.

Edmund A. Duggan
December 27, 1979
Page Three


Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A)); whether there is a nexus
between the governmental decision and the purpose for which
Mrs. Bridges receives income (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(B)); or whether the income, assets or gross
revenues of Madrone Associates itself will be affected by a
decision of the Planning Commission (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(3)(C)).

     In the situation you have described, it does not appear
a decision of the Planning Commission will have a direct
effect on Mrs. Bridges' income.  Mrs. Bridges has no interest
in the project under consideration and according to the
facts presented, her income from Madrone is in no way depen-
dent upon the Planning Commission's approval of projects for
which Madrone has acted as a consultant.  Secondly, because
Mrs. Bridges has not performed any consulting services for
Madrone on this project, there is no nexus between a decision
about it and the purpose for which she receives income.

     There remains for consideration the final question
whether Madrone Associates itself will be affected by the
decision.  Because part of the total product consultants
provide is often the ability to persuade a forum adopt a
certain position, the Commission has consistently looked to
the method of selecting the consultant to determine whether
a decision by a government official will have a financial
effect upon the consultant.  If a consultant were chosen,
for example, because of its consistent success before a
particular forum, it may fairly be said that a decision
maker in that forum is in a position to materially affect
the consultant.  When, however, the choice of a consultant
is made upon objective factors, it seems unlikely that a
decision of the forum would affect the letting of further
contracts.

     In this case, according to the facts as you have described
them, Madrone Associates was chosen by the City because of
its experience and expertise in the kind of biological inves-
tigations required for the wetlands study.  Any decision by
Mrs. Bridges would not affect the City's decision to again
contract with Madrone Associates since such decisions do not
take into account Madrone Associate's record before the
Planning Commission.  Therefore, Mrs. Bridges, as an occa-
sional independent contractor providing services for Madrone
Associates, would not be required to disqualify herself from

Edmund A. Duggan
December 27, 1979
Page Four


decisions in which Madrone has provided services to the
City. In reaching this conclusion, I wish to emphasize that
it is based upon the particular facts of this case. Questions
about Mrs. Bridges' disqualification with respect to decisions
involving Madrone Associates in other contexts, such as
those in which Madrone is chosen for its ability to obtain
an advantage for a private client, will require an independent
evaluation.

Sincerely,

Thomas M. Sobel
Chief, Legal Division

TMS:kp

CITY
OF
SAN RAFAEL

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY
EDMUND A. DUGGAN
City Attorney
WILLIAM J BULLARD JR
Assistant City Attorney

1400 FIFTH AVE, SAN RAFAEL, CALIF 94902/PHONE (415) 456-1112

MAYOR
LAWRENCE E MULRYAN
COUNCIL MEMBERS
DOROTHY L BREINER
FRED JENSEN
HENRY P MELLO
JACK G NIXON
CITY MANAGER
WILLIAM J BIELSER

December 7, 1979

Mr. Tom Sobel, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion - Possible Conflict of Interest -
Planning Commissioner, Cecilia Bridges

Dear Mr. Sobel:

Cecilia Bridges is a Planning Commissioner for San Rafael. She
has done work in the past year for Madrone Associates as a consultant and in this work she has earned more than $250. In
her work for Madrone Associates she has never undertaken any
project concerning the City of San Rafael. Madrone Associates
is involved with biological investigations for clients, including government agencies. Mrs. Bridges worked with Madrone Associates as a biologist. Madrone Associates has undertaken
work for the City of San Rafael, which work will be part of an
application to be acted upon by the Planning Commission upon
which Mrs. Bridges sits as a member.

The question is: When a matter comes before the Planning Commission in which Madrone Associates has provided input, does
this disqualify Mrs. Bridges even though she had never taken
part in any of the transactions associated with the matter
coming before the Commission.

We would appreciate a written answer on or before January 8,
1980. Thank you very much.

Sincerely,

EDMUND A. DUGGAN
City Attorney

cc: Ray Meador, Planning Director
Clifford Lund, Chairman, Planning Commission

19.2 B/27

sections.conclusion: Mrs. Bridges would not be disqualified from decisions in which Madrone provided services to the City because: (1) a Planning Commission decision would not have a direct effect on Mrs. Bridges' income; (2) there is no nexus between the decision and the purpose for which she receives income since she did not perform consulting services on the project; and (3) Madrone was selected based on objective criteria (experience and expertise) rather than its success before the Planning Commission, so the decision would not materially affect the consultant's business.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a Planning Commissioner with financial ties to a consulting firm (earning >$250/year) must disqualify herself from matters involving that firm when she personally did not work on the matter.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded Mrs. Bridges is not disqualified because the Planning Commission decision would not have a material financial effect on either her income or Madrone Associates' business, as Madrone was selected on objective qualifications rather than its ability to influence outcomes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with moderate quality; some text degradation and artifacts present. Response letter dated December 27, 1979 from FPPC to requesting attorney. Includes incoming request letter (December 7, 1979) at bottom providing case facts.

CONCLUSION: Mrs. Bridges would not be disqualified from decisions in which Madrone provided services to the City because: (1) a Planning Commission decision would not have a direct effect on Mrs. Bridges' income; (2) there is no nexus between the decision and the purpose for which she receives income since she did not perform consulting services on the project; and (3) Madrone was selected based on objective criteria (experience and expertise) rather than its success before the Planning Commission, so the decision would not materially affect the consultant's business.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter determining whether Planning Commissioner Cecilia Bridges must disqualify herself from matters involving consulting firm Madrone Associates, from which she receives income, when she personally did not work on the matter.
